title: MITRE ATT&CK — Reconnaissance (TA0043)
content:
TA0043 Reconnaissance covers adversary techniques for gathering information about a target before the breach. Reconnaissance is pre-compromise activity — it happens outside the victim's perimeter and is largely undetectable by traditional endpoint or network controls.

Key techniques:
- T1595 Active Scanning (sub: T1595.001 Scanning IP Blocks, T1595.002 Vulnerability Scanning, T1595.003 Wordlist Scanning) — adversary actively probes infrastructure; visible in WAF/IDS logs as port scans, vuln scans, or directory brute-forcing
- T1592 Gather Victim Host Information (sub: T1592.001 Hardware, T1592.002 Software, T1592.003 Firmware, T1592.004 Client Configurations) — passive collection of host details via job postings, vendor disclosures, conference talks
- T1589 Gather Victim Identity Information (sub: T1589.001 Credentials, T1589.002 Email Addresses, T1589.003 Employee Names) — LinkedIn scraping, breach data lookups, OSINT on identity
- T1590 Gather Victim Network Information (sub: T1590.001 Domain Properties, T1590.002 DNS, T1590.004 Network Topology, T1590.005 IP Addresses, T1590.006 Network Security Appliances) — WHOIS, DNS enumeration, Shodan/Censys queries
- T1591 Gather Victim Org Information (sub: T1591.001 Determine Physical Locations, T1591.002 Business Relationships, T1591.003 Identify Business Tempo, T1591.004 Identify Roles) — financial filings, news, vendor case studies
- T1593 Search Open Websites/Domains (sub: T1593.001 Social Media, T1593.002 Search Engines, T1593.003 Code Repositories)
- T1594 Search Victim-Owned Websites — careers pages, documentation portals
- T1596 Search Open Technical Databases (sub: T1596.001 DNS/Passive DNS, T1596.002 WHOIS, T1596.003 Digital Certificates, T1596.004 CDNs, T1596.005 Scan Databases)
- T1597 Search Closed Sources (sub: T1597.001 Threat Intel Vendors, T1597.002 Purchase Technical Data) — initial access broker forums, dark web marketplaces
- T1598 Phishing for Information (sub: T1598.001 Spearphishing Service, T1598.002 Spearphishing Attachment, T1598.003 Spearphishing Link, T1598.004 Spearphishing Voice) — info-gathering pretext, not yet payload delivery

Real-world adversary use: APT29 (Cozy Bear) extensively uses T1593.001 (social media) and T1589 (identity gathering) for targeting; ransomware affiliate groups (LockBit, BlackCat) routinely buy access enumeration from initial-access brokers (T1597.001) rather than scan themselves.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: reconnaissance is hard to detect because it's pre-perimeter. Defensive posture relies on (a) attack surface management (Bishop Fox, Censys, RiskIQ) to inventory what's exposed, (b) honeypots/canaries to detect scanning intent, (c) credential breach monitoring (Have I Been Pwned, SpyCloud) to detect when employee data leaks before phishing arrives. NIST CSF v2 maps reconnaissance defense to ID.RA-02 (cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources) and ID.AM-08 (assets are managed throughout their lifecycle).

Scope: pre-breach intelligence gathering. Use this when discussing attack surface management, OSINT defense, initial-access brokers, or why traditional defensive controls miss the earliest phase of a targeted attack.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Resource Development (TA0042)
content:
TA0042 Resource Development covers adversary techniques to establish resources they can use to support operations — infrastructure, accounts, capabilities, and malware acquired or developed before the actual intrusion.

Key techniques:
- T1583 Acquire Infrastructure (sub: T1583.001 Domains, T1583.002 DNS Server, T1583.003 Virtual Private Server, T1583.004 Server, T1583.005 Botnet, T1583.006 Web Services, T1583.007 Serverless, T1583.008 Malvertising) — adversary purchases or rents the infrastructure they'll use for C2, phishing landing pages, or staging
- T1584 Compromise Infrastructure (sub: T1584.001 Domains, T1584.002 DNS Server, T1584.003 VPS, T1584.004 Server, T1584.005 Botnet, T1584.006 Web Services, T1584.007 Serverless) — alternative to T1583: take over existing infrastructure to avoid attribution and reuse trusted reputation
- T1585 Establish Accounts (sub: T1585.001 Social Media, T1585.002 Email, T1585.003 Cloud) — fresh personas for social engineering, business email compromise staging
- T1586 Compromise Accounts (sub: T1586.001 Social Media, T1586.002 Email, T1586.003 Cloud) — credential-stuffing or phishing existing accounts to leverage their reputation
- T1587 Develop Capabilities (sub: T1587.001 Malware, T1587.002 Code Signing Certificates, T1587.003 Digital Certificates, T1587.004 Exploits) — bespoke tooling, often the signature of nation-state operations
- T1588 Obtain Capabilities (sub: T1588.001 Malware, T1588.002 Tool, T1588.003 Code Signing Certificates, T1588.004 Digital Certificates, T1588.005 Exploits, T1588.006 Vulnerabilities, T1588.007 Artificial Intelligence) — buying/borrowing tooling rather than building; the dominant pattern for cybercrime
- T1608 Stage Capabilities (sub: T1608.001 Upload Malware, T1608.002 Upload Tool, T1608.003 Install Digital Certificate, T1608.004 Drive-by Target, T1608.005 Link Target, T1608.006 SEO Poisoning) — adversary positions tooling on the infrastructure before launching attack
- T1650 Acquire Access — purchasing access from initial-access brokers (IABs); ransomware affiliate model relies on this

Real-world adversary use: ransomware groups (especially RaaS like LockBit, BlackCat, Akira) operationalize this tactic at scale — affiliates buy access via T1650, use T1583 to register lookalike domains for phishing, T1588.002 to obtain Cobalt Strike or Brute Ratel licenses. State actors (APT41, Sandworm) lean more on T1587 (developing custom capabilities) for stealth and long-dwell operations.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: most resource development happens outside the victim's network. Defense relies on (a) threat intelligence (DomainTools, Recorded Future) flagging newly registered lookalike domains, (b) certificate transparency log monitoring to detect adversary-issued certs targeting your brand, (c) IAB-marketplace monitoring (Flashpoint, Searchlight Cyber) for evidence your access is being sold. NIST CSF v2 maps this to ID.RA-02 (cyber threat intelligence is received) and DE.AE-07 (CTI integrated into analysis).

Scope: pre-intrusion preparation. Use this when discussing threat intel programs, brand-protection monitoring, certificate transparency, IAB ecosystems, or why a vendor like CrowdStrike Recon or Recorded Future provides value upstream of the breach.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Initial Access (TA0001)
content:
TA0001 Initial Access covers techniques adversaries use to gain their first foothold within a target network. This is the perimeter-piercing step, where almost all defensive investment lives.

Key techniques:
- T1566 Phishing (sub: T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment, T1566.002 Spearphishing Link, T1566.003 Spearphishing via Service, T1566.004 Spearphishing Voice) — the dominant initial access vector across 2023–2026 incident data
- T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application — Log4Shell, MOVEit, ConnectWise ScreenConnect, Citrix Bleed, Fortinet RCEs. The recurring pattern across the worst breaches of 2023–24
- T1133 External Remote Services — RDP and VPN access via stolen/brute-forced credentials. Heavily used by ransomware affiliates
- T1078 Valid Accounts (sub: T1078.001 Default Accounts, T1078.002 Domain Accounts, T1078.003 Local Accounts, T1078.004 Cloud Accounts) — using legitimate credentials obtained via phishing, infostealer logs, or IAB purchases; THE single most-used technique in breaches per Verizon DBIR 2026
- T1195 Supply Chain Compromise (sub: T1195.001 Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools, T1195.002 Compromise Software Supply Chain, T1195.003 Compromise Hardware Supply Chain) — SolarWinds, 3CX, Kaseya VSA, XZ Utils backdoor
- T1199 Trusted Relationship — compromising a managed service provider (MSP) or vendor to pivot into customer environments. The recurring CrowdStrike/Mandiant 2024 trend
- T1189 Drive-by Compromise — watering hole attacks; less common against enterprises in 2026 but persistent against journalists and activists
- T1200 Hardware Additions — USB drops, malicious cables (Ninja Cable). Niche but devastating in physical-access scenarios
- T1091 Replication Through Removable Media — USB worms; rare but persistent in OT environments

Real-world adversary patterns: per Verizon DBIR 2026, T1078 (Valid Accounts) appears in ~60% of breaches; T1566 (Phishing) in ~30%; T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) drives the largest blast-radius incidents. Ransomware affiliates strongly prefer T1078 + T1133 (valid accounts via VPN), often purchased from IABs at $500–$10,000 per access depending on company size and access depth.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: T1078 defense requires identity-centric controls — MFA enforcement (NIST CSF PR.AA-03), risk-based authentication, anomalous-login detection (Okta ITP, CrowdStrike Identity Protection). T1190 defense requires aggressive external attack surface management + patch SLAs measured in hours-to-days for internet-facing systems. T1195 defense requires SBOM discipline and CI/CD provenance (Sigstore, SLSA).

Scope: first foothold techniques. Use this when discussing perimeter defense investment, phishing-resistant MFA rollouts, external attack surface management, supply-chain security, MSP risk, or the relative cost of preventing each initial access vector vs the actual frequency in real breach data.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Execution (TA0002)
content:
TA0002 Execution covers techniques that result in adversary-controlled code running on a local or remote system. Execution is the post-foothold pivot point where EDR detection has its highest leverage.

Key techniques:
- T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter (sub: T1059.001 PowerShell, T1059.002 AppleScript, T1059.003 Windows Command Shell, T1059.004 Unix Shell, T1059.005 Visual Basic, T1059.006 Python, T1059.007 JavaScript, T1059.008 Network Device CLI, T1059.009 Cloud API, T1059.010 AutoHotKey & AutoIT, T1059.011 Lua) — PowerShell (T1059.001) is the single most-abused executor in Windows-based intrusions
- T1106 Native API — direct Windows API calls bypass scriptable-EDR hooks; favored by mature operators
- T1129 Shared Modules — DLL sideloading via legitimate Windows loader
- T1203 Exploitation for Client Execution — Office document exploits (Follina CVE-2022-30190 long tail), browser zero-days
- T1559 Inter-Process Communication (sub: T1559.001 COM, T1559.002 DDE, T1559.003 XPC) — abusing IPC for stealth
- T1053 Scheduled Task/Job (sub: T1053.002 At, T1053.003 Cron, T1053.005 Scheduled Task, T1053.006 Systemd Timers, T1053.007 Container Orchestration Job) — also doubles as persistence (TA0003)
- T1569 System Services (sub: T1569.001 Launchctl, T1569.002 Service Execution) — sc.exe / psexec patterns
- T1204 User Execution (sub: T1204.001 Malicious Link, T1204.002 Malicious File, T1204.003 Malicious Image) — relies on user double-click; the human-in-the-loop link
- T1047 Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) — fileless execution and lateral movement
- T1648 Serverless Execution — Lambda functions, Azure Functions as attacker compute
- T1610 Deploy Container — running malicious containers in compromised K8s clusters
- T1612 Build Image on Host — adversary builds container images to deploy in next step
- T1620 Reflective Code Loading — in-memory execution avoiding disk artifacts; classic Cobalt Strike beacon technique

Real-world adversary patterns: T1059.001 (PowerShell) was the most-prevalent execution technique across 2023–2024 incident response data. The 'living off the land' (LOLBins/LOLBAS) approach is overwhelming — adversaries prefer signed Microsoft binaries (rundll32, regsvr32, mshta, certutil) to avoid malware-on-disk detection. Ransomware affiliates routinely chain T1078 (Valid Accounts) → T1133 (External Remote Services) → T1059.001 (PowerShell) → T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) in <4 hours dwell time.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: this is where EDR/XDR earn their cost. PowerShell ScriptBlock logging (event 4104) + AMSI + EDR behavioral detection covers ~80% of T1059.001 abuse. Application allowlisting (WDAC, AppLocker, Cisco Secure Endpoint) addresses the LOLBins problem at the cost of significant operational friction. NIST CSF v2 maps execution defense to DE.CM-09 (computing hardware and software are monitored to find adverse events) and PR.PS-05 (installation and execution of unauthorized software are prevented).

Scope: code execution techniques. Use this when discussing EDR/XDR vendor selection (CrowdStrike vs SentinelOne vs Defender for Endpoint), PowerShell-restriction policies, application allowlisting trade-offs, or the role of behavioral detection vs signature-based AV.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Persistence (TA0003)
content:
TA0003 Persistence covers techniques adversaries use to maintain access across reboots, credential changes, and other interruptions. Persistence is the difference between a one-off incident and a sustained intrusion.

Key techniques:
- T1547 Boot or Logon Autostart Execution (sub: T1547.001 Registry Run Keys/Startup Folder, T1547.004 Winlogon Helper DLL, T1547.005 Security Support Provider, T1547.006 Kernel Modules and Extensions, T1547.009 Shortcut Modification, T1547.012 Print Processors, T1547.014 Active Setup, T1547.015 Login Items) — the legacy Windows persistence playbook
- T1543 Create or Modify System Process (sub: T1543.001 Launch Agent, T1543.002 Systemd Service, T1543.003 Windows Service, T1543.004 Launch Daemon) — service installation; visible in Sysmon event 4697
- T1136 Create Account (sub: T1136.001 Local Account, T1136.002 Domain Account, T1136.003 Cloud Account) — adversary-controlled accounts as a backdoor
- T1098 Account Manipulation (sub: T1098.001 Additional Cloud Credentials, T1098.002 Additional Email Delegate Permissions, T1098.003 Additional Cloud Roles, T1098.004 SSH Authorized Keys, T1098.005 Device Registration, T1098.006 Additional Container Cluster Roles, T1098.007 Additional Local or Domain Groups) — modify existing accounts (add MFA backdoor device, add OAuth app)
- T1505 Server Software Component (sub: T1505.001 SQL Stored Procedures, T1505.002 Transport Agent, T1505.003 Web Shell, T1505.004 IIS Components, T1505.005 Terminal Services DLL) — web shells remain the dominant persistence on perimeter Linux/Windows servers post T1190 exploit
- T1078 Valid Accounts — already in Initial Access; doubles as persistence when the account is long-lived
- T1574 Hijack Execution Flow (sub: T1574.001 DLL Search Order Hijacking, T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading, T1574.006 Dynamic Linker Hijacking, T1574.009 Path Interception by Unquoted Path, T1574.013 KernelCallbackTable, T1574.014 Authentication Package) — sophisticated, often paired with custom malware
- T1037 Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts (sub: T1037.001 Logon Script (Windows), T1037.003 Network Logon Script, T1037.005 Startup Items)
- T1546 Event Triggered Execution (sub: T1546.001 Change Default File Association, T1546.003 WMI Event Subscription, T1546.008 Accessibility Features, T1546.011 Application Shimming, T1546.013 PowerShell Profile, T1546.015 Component Object Model Hijacking)
- T1556 Modify Authentication Process (sub: T1556.001 Domain Controller Authentication, T1556.003 Pluggable Authentication Modules, T1556.005 Reversible Encryption, T1556.006 Multi-Factor Authentication, T1556.007 Hybrid Identity, T1556.008 Network Provider DLL) — the Skeleton Key class; AD-impacting and very high-severity
- T1554 Compromise Client Software Binary — replacing legit binaries with trojanized versions
- T1525 Implant Internal Image — backdoored VM images, container images, deployment templates

Real-world adversary patterns: ransomware groups typically establish 3–5 redundant persistence mechanisms before encrypting (so paid-ransom doesn't equal eviction); APT groups (especially Sandworm, APT29, Volt Typhoon) favor T1098.001 (additional cloud credentials) and T1078.004 (Valid Cloud Accounts) for resilient cloud persistence — surviving reimaging of every endpoint. Web shells (T1505.003) on Exchange and Citrix servers were the highest-impact persistence technique of 2024.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: persistence detection is mostly auditable from event logs but rarely watched in real time. Sysmon + EDR autoruns scanning catches the noisy techniques (T1547, T1543); cloud persistence (T1098.001) requires CSPM/CIEM tooling (Wiz, Orca, Permiso) because IAM changes don't show in endpoint telemetry. NIST CSF v2 maps persistence detection to DE.CM-09 and recovery to RC.RP-04 (post-incident operational norms — including persistence eviction).

Scope: maintain-access techniques. Use this when discussing identity-centric persistence (cloud roles, OAuth apps, MFA-backdoor), web shell detection on perimeter servers, the operational cost of full persistence eviction post-breach, or why cloud breach response is fundamentally different from endpoint response.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Privilege Escalation (TA0004)
content:
TA0004 Privilege Escalation covers techniques adversaries use to gain higher-level permissions on a system or network. PE is the bridge between 'we have a foothold' and 'we control the environment.'

Key techniques (many overlap with Persistence and Defense Evasion):
- T1078 Valid Accounts — when the initially-obtained account already has elevated privileges; the dominant PE in cloud breaches
- T1068 Exploitation for Privilege Escalation — kernel exploits (Print Nightmare CVE-2021-34527, PetitPotam, Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver / BYOVD against EDR drivers like RTCore64.sys)
- T1055 Process Injection (sub: T1055.001 DLL Injection, T1055.002 Portable Executable Injection, T1055.003 Thread Execution Hijacking, T1055.004 Asynchronous Procedure Call, T1055.008 Ptrace System Calls, T1055.009 Proc Memory, T1055.011 Extra Window Memory Injection, T1055.012 Process Hollowing, T1055.014 VDSO Hijacking, T1055.015 ListPlanting) — inject into a higher-privilege process; classic post-exploit move
- T1134 Access Token Manipulation (sub: T1134.001 Token Impersonation/Theft, T1134.002 Create Process with Token, T1134.003 Make and Impersonate Token, T1134.004 Parent PID Spoofing, T1134.005 SID-History Injection)
- T1574 Hijack Execution Flow — DLL search-order, sideloading, COM hijacking
- T1543 Create or Modify System Process — service installation often runs as SYSTEM
- T1547 Boot or Logon Autostart Execution — similar overlap
- T1548 Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism (sub: T1548.001 Setuid/Setgid, T1548.002 Bypass UAC, T1548.003 Sudo and Sudo Caching, T1548.004 Elevated Execution with Prompt, T1548.005 Temporary Elevated Cloud Access, T1548.006 TCC Manipulation)
- T1611 Escape to Host — container escape; common in K8s compromise scenarios
- T1037 Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts
- T1098 Account Manipulation — adding self to privileged groups (Domain Admins, Cloud Admin roles)

Real-world adversary patterns: most enterprise PE in 2024–2026 is identity-based, not exploit-based — adversary gets a valid account via phishing/IAB, then performs Kerberoasting (T1558.003) or AS-REP Roasting (T1558.004) to escalate within Active Directory. In cloud (especially AWS), the dominant PE path is T1098.001 (Additional Cloud Credentials) chained with role assumption abuse — adversary creates new access keys or assumes overly-permissive roles. Volt Typhoon's 2023–24 campaigns against US critical infrastructure heavily used T1078 + T1098 for credential-led PE without ever dropping malware.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: BYOVD attacks against EDR drivers are the major 2024–25 trend — defending requires Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist enforcement (HVCI) + EDR vendor's own driver-tampering protection (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender all rolled out blocklists in 2024). For cloud PE, the answer is least-privilege IAM (impossible to enforce manually at scale; requires CIEM tooling — Wiz, Permiso, Sonrai). For AD PE, Microsoft's Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) + tier-0 admin model + Just-in-Time admin access (Microsoft PIM, CyberArk PAM) are the proven controls. NIST CSF v2 maps PE defense to PR.AA-05 (least privilege) and DE.CM-09.

Scope: gaining elevated permissions. Use this when discussing PAM/PIM investments, CIEM tools for cloud least-privilege, AD tiering models, the BYOVD epidemic, EDR driver hardening, or why identity-based PE has overtaken exploit-based PE in enterprise breach data.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Defense Evasion (TA0005)
content:
TA0005 Defense Evasion covers techniques adversaries use to avoid detection throughout the attack lifecycle. DE is the largest tactic in ATT&CK by technique count, reflecting how much engineering effort adversaries put into staying invisible.

Key techniques:
- T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information (sub: T1027.001 Binary Padding, T1027.002 Software Packing, T1027.003 Steganography, T1027.004 Compile After Delivery, T1027.005 Indicator Removal from Tools, T1027.006 HTML Smuggling, T1027.007 Dynamic API Resolution, T1027.008 Stripped Payloads, T1027.009 Embedded Payloads, T1027.010 Command Obfuscation, T1027.011 Fileless Storage, T1027.012 LNK Icon Smuggling, T1027.013 Encrypted/Encoded File, T1027.014 Polymorphic Code, T1027.015 Compression)
- T1070 Indicator Removal (sub: T1070.001 Clear Windows Event Logs, T1070.002 Clear Linux or Mac System Logs, T1070.003 Clear Command History, T1070.004 File Deletion, T1070.005 Network Share Connection Removal, T1070.006 Timestomp, T1070.007 Clear Network Connection History, T1070.008 Clear Mailbox Data, T1070.009 Clear Persistence) — log clearing is a major IR forensic friction point
- T1562 Impair Defenses (sub: T1562.001 Disable or Modify Tools, T1562.002 Disable Windows Event Logging, T1562.004 Disable or Modify System Firewall, T1562.006 Indicator Blocking, T1562.007 Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall, T1562.008 Disable or Modify Cloud Logs, T1562.010 Downgrade Attack) — disabling EDR before encryption is the dominant ransomware pre-step
- T1218 System Binary Proxy Execution (sub: T1218.001 Compiled HTML File, T1218.005 Mshta, T1218.007 Msiexec, T1218.010 Regsvr32, T1218.011 Rundll32, T1218.013 Mavinject, T1218.014 MMC) — LOLBins
- T1055 Process Injection — also PE; also evasion via running malicious code in trusted process context
- T1036 Masquerading (sub: T1036.001 Invalid Code Signature, T1036.003 Rename System Utilities, T1036.004 Masquerade Task or Service, T1036.005 Match Legitimate Name or Location, T1036.006 Space after Filename, T1036.007 Double File Extension, T1036.008 Masquerade File Type, T1036.009 Break Process Trees)
- T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
- T1497 Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion (sub: T1497.001 System Checks, T1497.002 User Activity Based Checks, T1497.003 Time Based Evasion) — sandbox evasion to bypass automated malware analysis
- T1620 Reflective Code Loading — in-memory; no disk artifact for EDR to scan
- T1480 Execution Guardrails (sub: T1480.001 Environmental Keying) — payloads that only execute on the intended target
- T1622 Debugger Evasion
- T1553 Subvert Trust Controls (sub: T1553.001 Gatekeeper Bypass, T1553.002 Code Signing, T1553.003 SIP and Trust Provider Hijacking, T1553.004 Install Root Certificate, T1553.005 Mark-of-the-Web Bypass, T1553.006 Code Signing Policy Modification)
- T1564 Hide Artifacts (sub: T1564.001 Hidden Files and Directories, T1564.003 Hidden Window, T1564.004 NTFS File Attributes, T1564.006 Run Virtual Instance, T1564.008 Email Hiding Rules, T1564.009 Resource Forking, T1564.010 Process Argument Spoofing, T1564.011 Ignore Process Interrupts, T1564.012 File/Path Exclusions)

Real-world adversary patterns: T1562.001 (disabling EDR) is the single most-investigated evasion technique in 2024–2025 IR data. BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) — using a signed-but-vulnerable driver to kill EDR processes from kernel space — featured in the LockBit, BlackCat, and Akira playbooks. T1027.006 (HTML smuggling) was the dominant initial-stage evasion of 2023–24, replacing macro-armed Office documents after Microsoft disabled macros by default in 2022.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: EDR tamper protection (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender for Endpoint, Microsoft Defender XDR) is the table-stakes control. Centralized log forwarding to immutable SIEM storage (Splunk Cloud, Sentinel) defeats T1070 (Indicator Removal). Cloud audit log immutability (CloudTrail with object-lock S3, Azure Monitor with immutability settings) defeats T1562.008 (Disable Cloud Logs). NIST CSF v2 maps defense-evasion mitigations to PR.PS-01 (configuration management), DE.CM-09 (continuous monitoring), and RC.RP-03 (backup integrity).

Scope: detection avoidance. Use this when discussing EDR tamper-protection, SIEM log immutability, BYOVD defense, sandbox-evasion in detonation chambers (Joe Sandbox, ANY.RUN), or why EDR alone is insufficient without immutable log forwarding.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Credential Access (TA0006)
content:
TA0006 Credential Access covers techniques adversaries use to steal account credentials. Per Verizon DBIR 2026, credential theft is the most common precursor to lateral movement and one of the top-three causes of major breaches.

Key techniques:
- T1003 OS Credential Dumping (sub: T1003.001 LSASS Memory, T1003.002 Security Account Manager, T1003.003 NTDS, T1003.004 LSA Secrets, T1003.005 Cached Domain Credentials, T1003.006 DCSync, T1003.007 Proc Filesystem, T1003.008 /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow) — Mimikatz / Pypykatz family; LSASS dumping is the canonical Windows AD attack
- T1110 Brute Force (sub: T1110.001 Password Guessing, T1110.002 Password Cracking, T1110.003 Password Spraying, T1110.004 Credential Stuffing) — password spraying (low-and-slow against many accounts) defeats lockout policies
- T1555 Credentials from Password Stores (sub: T1555.001 Keychain, T1555.002 Securityd Memory, T1555.003 Credentials from Web Browsers, T1555.004 Windows Credential Manager, T1555.005 Password Managers, T1555.006 Cloud Secrets Management Stores) — infostealer malware (RedLine, Raccoon, Vidar, Lumma) operationalizes this at scale
- T1558 Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets (sub: T1558.001 Golden Ticket, T1558.002 Silver Ticket, T1558.003 Kerberoasting, T1558.004 AS-REP Roasting, T1558.005 Ccache Files) — Kerberoasting (T1558.003) is the dominant AD post-foothold escalation path
- T1552 Unsecured Credentials (sub: T1552.001 Credentials In Files, T1552.002 Credentials in Registry, T1552.004 Private Keys, T1552.005 Cloud Instance Metadata API, T1552.006 Group Policy Preferences, T1552.007 Container API, T1552.008 Chat Messages) — secrets in Git history (TruffleHog, GitGuardian land here), in S3 buckets, in instance metadata services. AWS IMDSv1 was the canonical 'cloud breach pattern' through 2023; IMDSv2 mostly fixed it
- T1056 Input Capture (sub: T1056.001 Keylogging, T1056.002 GUI Input Capture, T1056.003 Web Portal Capture, T1056.004 Credential API Hooking)
- T1539 Steal Web Session Cookie — session-token theft via infostealers bypasses MFA when SSO sessions are long-lived. The Lapsus$ playbook. Largely the cause of the Okta 2022 and Snowflake 2024 incidents
- T1606 Forge Web Credentials (sub: T1606.001 Web Cookies, T1606.002 SAML Tokens) — Golden SAML (Solorigate / SolarWinds); a nation-state signature
- T1187 Forced Authentication — NTLM relay; PetitPotam; Coercer
- T1556 Modify Authentication Process — also Persistence; Skeleton Key class; MFA bypass via authentication package modification
- T1621 Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation — MFA fatigue / push-bombing; how Uber 2022 fell
- T1649 Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates — AD CS abuse (Certified Pre-Owned / ESC1–8)

Real-world adversary patterns: T1003.001 (LSASS dumping via Mimikatz) and T1558.003 (Kerberoasting) remain the AD-side bread and butter despite years of detection investment. T1539 (session cookie theft via infostealer) is the rising 2024–2026 pattern — bypasses MFA at scale because the stolen session token IS already-authenticated. Snowflake 2024 incident affecting AT&T, Ticketmaster, and others tied to infostealer-driven session theft on Snowflake accounts without MFA enforced.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: LSASS protection (Credential Guard, RunAsPPL) blocks T1003.001 at OS level; EDR tamper protection prevents adversary from disabling it. Kerberoasting defense requires strong service-account passwords (>25 chars, randomly generated) — most orgs fail this. Session-theft defense requires phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn, not SMS or push), short session TTLs, and device-bound tokens (token-binding, DPoP). Cloud-secrets defense requires secrets management (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) + scanning (GitGuardian, TruffleHog). NIST CSF v2 maps to PR.AA-03 (authentication), PR.AA-05 (least privilege), PR.DS-01 (data at rest, including credential stores), DE.CM-09.

Scope: credential theft. Use this when discussing FIDO2 rollouts, infostealer defense, Snowflake-style MFA bypass scenarios, AD hardening (LSASS protection, Kerberoasting defense), secrets management, AD CS hardening, or why MFA alone is no longer sufficient against session-theft attacks.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Discovery (TA0007)
content:
TA0007 Discovery covers techniques adversaries use to gain knowledge of the victim environment — what's there, who has access, where the crown jewels are. Discovery is usually noisy and detectable, making it the highest-leverage tactic for behavioral SOC detection.

Key techniques:
- T1057 Process Discovery — tasklist, ps; visible in command-line telemetry
- T1083 File and Directory Discovery — dir, ls, find; high signal when an adversary touches many directories rapidly
- T1018 Remote System Discovery — net view, nltest, BloodHound's SharpHound collector
- T1087 Account Discovery (sub: T1087.001 Local Account, T1087.002 Domain Account, T1087.003 Email Account, T1087.004 Cloud Account) — net user, Get-ADUser, Azure AD enumeration
- T1069 Permission Groups Discovery (sub: T1069.001 Local Groups, T1069.002 Domain Groups, T1069.003 Cloud Groups) — net group; AD privileged-group enumeration is the canonical BloodHound query path
- T1482 Domain Trust Discovery — nltest /domain_trusts; cross-domain pivot planning
- T1016 System Network Configuration Discovery (sub: T1016.001 Internet Connection Discovery, T1016.002 Wi-Fi Discovery) — ipconfig, route
- T1049 System Network Connections Discovery — netstat
- T1033 System Owner/User Discovery — whoami; the most-canonical post-foothold first command
- T1082 System Information Discovery — systeminfo, uname; runs early in nearly every breach
- T1135 Network Share Discovery — net share, net view
- T1518 Software Discovery (sub: T1518.001 Security Software Discovery) — wmic product, Get-Service; T1518.001 specifically targets identifying what EDR/AV is installed before Defense Evasion
- T1046 Network Service Discovery — port scanning from inside; visible in IDS / EDR network telemetry
- T1124 System Time Discovery — adversary times exfil to off-hours
- T1010 Application Window Discovery
- T1614 System Location Discovery (sub: T1614.001 System Language Discovery) — payload only fires on intended geography
- T1217 Browser Bookmark Discovery — pivot to internal SaaS apps
- T1538 Cloud Service Dashboard — adversary uses victim cloud console for recon
- T1526 Cloud Service Discovery — enumerate AWS services in use
- T1580 Cloud Infrastructure Discovery — list EC2 instances, S3 buckets
- T1619 Cloud Storage Object Discovery — S3 bucket enumeration
- T1652 Device Driver Discovery — adversary inventories drivers for BYOVD selection

Real-world adversary patterns: BloodHound (and SharpHound collector) operationalizes T1087.002 + T1069.002 + T1482 in a single sweep — the AD enumeration step is now <5 minutes from foothold to 'show me the shortest path to Domain Admin.' Cloud discovery (T1526, T1580, T1619) is the canonical post-T1098.001 step in cloud breaches: enumerate roles, list S3 buckets, find IAM keys.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: discovery activity has the highest noise-to-detection-cost ratio — these commands are run thousands of times by IT admins, but rarely in sequence by a single user from a single endpoint within a short window. SIEM correlation rules (a single non-admin endpoint running 5+ AD enumeration commands in 5 minutes) are the high-value detections. BloodHound execution itself can be detected by EDR (Windows Event 4662 + LDAP query patterns; Microsoft Sentinel ships native detections). NIST CSF v2 maps to DE.CM-01 (network monitoring) and DE.AE-03 (correlation from multiple sources).

Scope: environment enumeration. Use this when discussing BloodHound defense, SIEM correlation rule investment, why discovery is the highest-leverage SOC detection point, cloud IAM enumeration (Pacu, ScoutSuite from offensive side; Wiz, Permiso from defensive side), or how to harden AD against the canonical BloodHound query path.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Lateral Movement (TA0008)
content:
TA0008 Lateral Movement covers techniques adversaries use to pivot from one system to another within the victim environment. LM is the step where blast radius grows from a single endpoint to systemic compromise.

Key techniques:
- T1021 Remote Services (sub: T1021.001 Remote Desktop Protocol, T1021.002 SMB/Windows Admin Shares, T1021.003 Distributed Component Object Model, T1021.004 SSH, T1021.005 VNC, T1021.006 Windows Remote Management, T1021.007 Cloud Services, T1021.008 Direct Cloud VM Connections) — RDP (T1021.001) and SMB admin shares (T1021.002) are the dominant Windows LM paths; SSH (T1021.004) for Linux
- T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer — copying Cobalt Strike beacon to next host
- T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material (sub: T1550.001 Application Access Token, T1550.002 Pass the Hash, T1550.003 Pass the Ticket, T1550.004 Web Session Cookie) — Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket are the post-Mimikatz pivot techniques
- T1563 Remote Service Session Hijacking (sub: T1563.001 SSH Hijacking, T1563.002 RDP Hijacking)
- T1534 Internal Spearphishing — pivot to internal targets using compromised internal account; bypasses external email gateways
- T1080 Taint Shared Content — putting malicious payload in a shared drive
- T1210 Exploitation of Remote Services — EternalBlue-style worming; rare in mature environments but devastating where it works
- T1072 Software Deployment Tools — abusing SCCM, Intune, ManageEngine to push payloads enterprise-wide
- T1091 Replication Through Removable Media — niche, but critical in OT/ICS environments
- T1098.001 Additional Cloud Credentials — also Persistence; in cloud breaches this IS lateral movement

Real-world adversary patterns: ransomware affiliates routinely move from initial-access endpoint to Domain Controller in <4 hours via T1078 (valid accounts) + T1021.002 (SMB) + T1550.002 (Pass-the-Hash) using PsExec or PowerShell remoting. Cloud lateral movement is fundamentally different: adversary assumes one role via T1098.001, enumerates trust relationships via T1538/T1526, then assumes target role — no 'network' lateral movement, just IAM-graph traversal. AssumeRole chaining in AWS is the canonical cloud LM technique; Wiz's Security Graph and Orca's attack-path analysis target this specifically.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: network segmentation (NIST CSF PR.IR-01) is the structural defense — east-west microsegmentation (Illumio, Akamai Guardicore, VMware NSX) limits lateral blast radius. Tier-0 admin isolation in AD (separate admin workstations, JIT access via PIM/CyberArk) defeats T1550. Behavioral SOC detection on anomalous RDP/SMB patterns (lateral from non-admin endpoint to multiple internal IPs) is the SIEM-side defense. For cloud, the answer is least-privilege IAM, role-assumption monitoring (CloudTrail AssumeRole events), and graph-based attack-path detection (Wiz, Orca, Permiso, Sonrai). NIST CSF v2 maps to PR.AA-05 (least privilege), PR.IR-01 (network protection), DE.CM-01 (network monitoring), DE.AE-03 (correlation).

Scope: pivot techniques. Use this when discussing microsegmentation investments (Illumio vs Akamai vs Cisco Secure Workload), tier-0 admin model, JIT/PIM/PAM, cloud IAM graph analysis (Wiz vs Orca vs Permiso), or why traditional 'east-west firewall' approaches fail in modern cloud-first environments.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Collection (TA0009)
content:
TA0009 Collection covers techniques adversaries use to gather information of interest before exfiltration. Collection is the last reversible step before data leaves the environment — DLP and behavioral monitoring have their last shot here.

Key techniques:
- T1560 Archive Collected Data (sub: T1560.001 Archive via Utility, T1560.002 Archive via Library, T1560.003 Archive via Custom Method) — adversary compresses data before exfil; 7z and rar are the canonical patterns and very detectable
- T1119 Automated Collection — scripted data gathering
- T1115 Clipboard Data
- T1213 Data from Information Repositories (sub: T1213.001 Confluence, T1213.002 SharePoint, T1213.003 Code Repositories, T1213.004 Customer Relationship Management Software, T1213.005 Messaging Applications) — pivoting to internal wikis, code repos, ticketing systems. T1213.003 (code repos) is how IP theft most often happens
- T1005 Data from Local System — file system scanning
- T1039 Data from Network Shared Drive
- T1025 Data from Removable Media
- T1530 Data from Cloud Storage Object — S3 buckets, Azure Blob, GCS
- T1602 Data from Configuration Repository (sub: T1602.001 SNMP MIB Dump, T1602.002 Network Device Configuration Dump)
- T1074 Data Staged (sub: T1074.001 Local Data Staging, T1074.002 Remote Data Staging) — adversary collects across many systems into a single staging location before exfil
- T1114 Email Collection (sub: T1114.001 Local Email Collection, T1114.002 Remote Email Collection, T1114.003 Email Forwarding Rule) — mail-forwarding rules (T1114.003) are silent and persistent; the canonical BEC technique
- T1056 Input Capture — also Credential Access
- T1185 Browser Session Hijacking — manipulating browser to access internal apps
- T1113 Screen Capture
- T1123 Audio Capture
- T1125 Video Capture
- T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle (sub: T1557.001 LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay, T1557.002 ARP Cache Poisoning, T1557.003 DHCP Spoofing) — internal MITM

Real-world adversary patterns: ransomware groups now routinely exfil before encrypt (the 'double extortion' model) — collection happens over days/weeks, often via Rclone or MEGAcmd uploading to Mega.nz or Anonfiles. APT groups exfil more slowly and selectively, with collection happening over months. BEC fraud uses T1114.003 (email forwarding rules) almost universally to monitor target inboxes for invoice changes.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: collection is the highest-yield detection point for DLP and UEBA tooling. Anomalous file-access patterns (a user reading 10,000 files in an hour), large local stages of archives, and unusual mail-forwarding rule creations are all high-signal detections. NIST CSF v2 maps to DE.CM-09 (continuous monitoring of computing assets) and PR.DS-01 (data at rest protection — labeling sensitive data so DLP can see it). DLP vendors (Microsoft Purview, Symantec, Forcepoint, Netskope) live and die on collection-stage detection.

Scope: pre-exfil data gathering. Use this when discussing DLP vendor selection, mail-forwarding-rule monitoring (the BEC defense), insider-threat detection, code repository monitoring (GitGuardian, GitHub Advanced Security), or why double-extortion ransomware shifted the collection-stage detection problem from 'rare APT scenario' to 'every-ransomware-incident scenario.'

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Command and Control (TA0011)
content:
TA0011 Command and Control covers techniques adversaries use to communicate with compromised systems while maintaining stealth. C2 is the persistent channel between the operator and the implant — every modern intrusion has one.

Key techniques:
- T1071 Application Layer Protocol (sub: T1071.001 Web Protocols, T1071.002 File Transfer Protocols, T1071.003 Mail Protocols, T1071.004 DNS, T1071.005 Publish/Subscribe Protocols) — HTTP/HTTPS (T1071.001) is the dominant C2 channel because it blends with normal traffic
- T1573 Encrypted Channel (sub: T1573.001 Symmetric Cryptography, T1573.002 Asymmetric Cryptography) — TLS-encrypted C2; bypasses content-inspection unless TLS interception is in place
- T1572 Protocol Tunneling — encapsulating one protocol in another (e.g. SSH over HTTPS)
- T1090 Proxy (sub: T1090.001 Internal Proxy, T1090.002 External Proxy, T1090.003 Multi-hop Proxy, T1090.004 Domain Fronting) — Cloudflare/Fastly fronting was patched by major CDNs in 2018–2020 but other front patterns persist
- T1568 Dynamic Resolution (sub: T1568.001 Fast Flux DNS, T1568.002 Domain Generation Algorithms, T1568.003 DNS Calculation)
- T1102 Web Service (sub: T1102.001 Dead Drop Resolver, T1102.002 Bidirectional Communication, T1102.003 One-Way Communication) — abusing legitimate services (Discord, Slack, GitHub, Pastebin, Telegram) as C2 — defeats domain-reputation defenses
- T1095 Non-Application Layer Protocol — raw sockets, ICMP tunneling
- T1132 Data Encoding (sub: T1132.001 Standard Encoding, T1132.002 Non-Standard Encoding) — base64, xor
- T1001 Data Obfuscation (sub: T1001.001 Junk Data, T1001.002 Steganography, T1001.003 Protocol or Service Impersonation)
- T1008 Fallback Channels
- T1104 Multi-Stage Channels — stager retrieves second-stage payload from different infrastructure
- T1219 Remote Access Software — abuse of legitimate RMM tools (AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect, Atera) as adversary C2. Massive trend 2023–2025 — RMM-as-C2 bypasses endpoint security because the RMM agent IS legitimately signed and trusted
- T1659 Content Injection — adversary modifies legitimate web content to deliver next stage
- T1665 Hide Infrastructure — domain rotation, IP rotation

Real-world adversary patterns: Cobalt Strike remains the most-used C2 framework (commercial, leaked); Brute Ratel C4 grew rapidly 2022–24 as a Cobalt Strike alternative; Sliver (open source, BishopFox) is the third major option. RMM-as-C2 (T1219) — using ScreenConnect, AnyDesk, or Atera as the adversary's persistent remote-control channel — drove the bulk of ransomware incidents 2023–2025 because the RMM agents bypass EDR alerting (they're allowlisted). Volt Typhoon's living-off-the-land approach against US critical infrastructure leans heavily on T1071.001 + T1573 with no custom malware, making detection extremely difficult.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: TLS-encrypted C2 is invisible to traditional content inspection — defense relies on (a) TLS interception at perimeter (cost, complexity, privacy debates), (b) JA3/JA4 client fingerprinting to identify Cobalt Strike / Sliver / Brute Ratel traffic, (c) behavioral network telemetry (Zeek, Corelight) detecting anomalous connection patterns (beacon timing, jitter, dest IP entropy), (d) DNS analytics for DGA detection (Cisco Umbrella, Infoblox). RMM-as-C2 defense requires an allowlist of approved RMM tools (block any unapproved RMM install at EDR level) + monitoring approved RMM logs for anomalous sessions. NIST CSF v2 maps to DE.CM-01 (network monitoring) and PR.IR-01 (network protection).

Scope: adversary communications. Use this when discussing C2 framework detection (Cobalt Strike, Brute Ratel, Sliver), TLS interception trade-offs, JA3/JA4 fingerprinting, DNS analytics tooling, RMM-tool allowlisting policies, or why network detection has shifted from signature-based to behavioral.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Exfiltration (TA0010)
content:
TA0010 Exfiltration covers techniques adversaries use to steal data from the victim network. Exfil is the irreversible point — once data leaves, it's gone. In double-extortion ransomware, exfil happens BEFORE encryption.

Key techniques:
- T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel — adversary reuses existing C2 channel for data; common but volumetrically constrained
- T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol (sub: T1048.001 Exfiltration Over Symmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol, T1048.002 Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol, T1048.003 Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol)
- T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service (sub: T1567.001 Exfiltration to Code Repository, T1567.002 Exfiltration to Cloud Storage, T1567.003 Exfiltration to Text Storage Sites, T1567.004 Exfiltration Over Webhook) — Mega.nz, Anonfiles, Dropbox, Google Drive, GitHub gists. The dominant ransomware exfil pattern in 2024–2026 because these services are reputation-trusted by every proxy
- T1029 Scheduled Transfer — exfil during off-hours
- T1030 Data Transfer Size Limits — adversary chunks data to evade DLP volume-based alerts
- T1011 Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium (sub: T1011.001 Exfiltration Over Bluetooth)
- T1052 Exfiltration Over Physical Medium (sub: T1052.001 Exfiltration over USB) — insider scenarios primarily
- T1020 Automated Exfiltration (sub: T1020.001 Traffic Duplication)
- T1537 Transfer Data to Cloud Account — adversary uses target's own cloud account or attacker-controlled cloud (S3, Azure Blob) as exfil destination; native cloud APIs bypass DLP

Real-world adversary patterns: Rclone (open source cloud-sync tool) is the universal ransomware exfil tool — works against S3, Azure Blob, GCS, Mega.nz, Dropbox, OneDrive, dozens more. MEGAcmd, FileZilla, WinSCP appear in IR data with high frequency. The shift from FTP/SFTP exfil to T1567.002 (cloud storage) is near-complete — adversaries prefer cloud APIs because they bypass perimeter proxy inspection (TLS, trusted CDN reputation, normal employee traffic patterns). Snowflake 2024 incident: exfil happened via legitimate Snowflake API calls from compromised customer accounts; no traditional network detection could distinguish adversary queries from legitimate analytics.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: exfiltration detection requires DLP that operates on egress flows (Netskope, Zscaler, Microsoft Purview, Forcepoint) with policy distinguishing high-volume reads from sensitive sources. Cloud-native detection (CASB or CSPM with data-flow monitoring) catches T1537 / T1567.002. Network egress monitoring for anomalous transfers to cloud storage hostnames (rclone signatures, MEGAcmd patterns) is the EDR/firewall-side defense. Egress to known IAB exfil destinations (specific Anonfiles patterns, Bunkr, file.io) should be hard-blocked at the proxy. NIST CSF v2 maps to PR.DS-02 (data in transit), DE.CM-01 (network monitoring), DE.AE-03 (correlation).

The HARD truth from real IR data: exfiltration is detected post-fact in the majority of double-extortion ransomware cases — by the time the SOC sees 200GB egress to mega.nz, the data is already out. The defensive shift over 2024–2025 has been toward 'limit blast radius' (encryption at rest, tokenization, narrow IAM scopes on data stores) so that 'data exfil happened' doesn't equal 'data is readable' — Vaultree, Skyflow, and HashiCorp Vault Transit-encryption patterns address this.

Scope: data theft. Use this when discussing DLP vendor selection (Netskope vs Zscaler vs Microsoft Purview), cloud egress monitoring, Rclone signature detection, the 'limit blast radius' shift to tokenization/encryption, or why traditional perimeter DLP fails against modern cloud-API-based exfil.

title: MITRE ATT&CK — Impact (TA0040)
content:
TA0040 Impact covers techniques adversaries use to manipulate, interrupt, or destroy systems and data. Impact is the monetization step — ransomware, wiper attacks, defacement, fraud, denial of service.

Key techniques:
- T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact — ransomware encryption; the dominant impact technique 2023–2026. Major variants: LockBit, ALPHV/BlackCat, Akira, Play, RansomHub, Hunters International, Medusa, Qilin
- T1485 Data Destruction — wiper attacks (NotPetya legacy; recent: HermeticWiper, AcidRain, IsaacWiper used against Ukrainian targets by Sandworm)
- T1565 Data Manipulation (sub: T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation, T1565.002 Transmitted Data Manipulation, T1565.003 Runtime Data Manipulation) — financial fraud, wire-transfer manipulation, blood-bank-record alteration
- T1490 Inhibit System Recovery — deleting volume shadow copies (vssadmin delete shadows), disabling Windows recovery (bcdedit /set), wiping backups. The pre-ransomware step that defeats traditional 'just restore from backup' defense
- T1491 Defacement (sub: T1491.001 Internal Defacement, T1491.002 External Defacement) — hacktivist / public-pressure scenarios
- T1561 Disk Wipe (sub: T1561.001 Disk Content Wipe, T1561.002 Disk Structure Wipe)
- T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service (sub: T1499.001 OS Exhaustion Flood, T1499.002 Service Exhaustion Flood, T1499.003 Application Exhaustion Flood, T1499.004 Application or System Exploitation)
- T1498 Network Denial of Service (sub: T1498.001 Direct Network Flood, T1498.002 Reflection Amplification)
- T1495 Firmware Corruption — rare but devastating; BlackEnergy 3 against Ukrainian grid 2015
- T1531 Account Access Removal — adversary locks legitimate admins out by changing passwords or disabling accounts; common in ransomware to prevent response
- T1657 Financial Theft — direct theft (BEC fraud, crypto wallet drains)
- T1496 Resource Hijacking — cryptomining, abusing victim cloud bills (XMRig); SCARLETEEL pattern stealing AWS credentials for mining
- T1489 Service Stop — disabling DBMS / web servers before encryption

Real-world adversary patterns: T1486 (ransomware encryption) preceded by T1490 (inhibit system recovery — shadow copy deletion) appears in essentially every ransomware incident. Pure-wiper attacks (T1485 / T1561) are predominantly state-actor (Sandworm against Ukraine, Iran-attributed actors against Israel). Cryptomining (T1496) is the dominant 'opportunistic' cloud impact — every exposed Kubernetes / Docker / Jenkins gets hit eventually. Financial theft via BEC (T1657 chained from T1114.003 email forwarding rules) remains the highest-dollar non-ransomware fraud vector per FBI IC3 data.

Detection guidance for CISO panel reasoning: impact-stage detection is too late — defense relies on backup architecture (immutable, air-gapped, tested per NIST CSF RC.RP-03), recovery time objective discipline, and incident response retainers (DFIR firms — CrowdStrike Services, Mandiant, Kroll, Arete) for the cases where impact happens. Ransomware containment relies on network segmentation (limit blast radius), tier-0 admin model (prevent encryption of DCs), and EDR rollback capabilities (CrowdStrike Falcon Insight, SentinelOne Singularity rollback). For cryptomining, CSPM tooling (Wiz, Orca) catches anomalous cloud workload behavior. NIST CSF v2 maps to RC.RP-01 through RC.RP-06 (recovery plan execution), RS.MI-01 (incident containment), and PR.IR-03 (resilience requirements).

The hardest CISO conversation in 2024–2026: ransomware payment decision. FBI / OFAC stance is 'don't pay' (sanctions risk with OFAC-listed groups — LockBit attributed to OFAC sanctions September 2024); reality is many orgs pay because backups failed RC.RP-03 testing. The right CISO posture is to test backups quarterly with full restore drills so the payment decision never has to be made under duress.

Scope: monetization / destruction. Use this when discussing ransomware preparedness, backup architecture, DFIR retainers, payment decision frameworks, OFAC sanctions context, network segmentation for blast-radius limitation, or why 'just restore from backup' fails as a ransomware defense when T1490 has destroyed the backups.
